title: Check yourself: meaning & structure
questions.0.q: Four paragraphs. Three are listings, one is a note. They want the three in a grey box, the note not. Why can they not?
questions.0.options.0: Because paragraphs cannot have a background
questions.0.options.1:
Because all four are

and there is nothing to tell them apart. You cannot change what you cannot point at
questions.0.options.2: Because they must learn CSS first
questions.0.options.3: They can — they just need to reorder them
questions.0.explain: A class names a group. Give the three listings class="listing" and you can now say "everything with that name goes in a grey box", leaving the note alone.
questions.1.q: They are naming a class for a box that is currently grey. Which name is better?
questions.1.options.0: class="grey-box" — it says exactly what it is
questions.1.options.1: class="listing" — it says what the thing IS, not how it looks today
questions.1.options.3: It makes no difference, as long as it works
questions.1.explain: Tomorrow you decide the box should be blue. The class is called "grey". Either you rename it everywhere, or you live with a lie in your code. "listing" never goes stale — a listing stays a listing.
questions.2.options.0: Always — it is universal and causes no trouble
questions.2.options.1: Never — <div> is forbidden in modern HTML
questions.2.options.2: When there is NO tag that says what the thing is. Then it is the last resort
questions.2.explain: There is no "logo" tag. No "layout box" tag. There <div> belongs. But a heading has <h1>, a menu has <nav>, a listing has <article>. Ask yourself: is there a tag that says what this is?
questions.3.q: They want a word in the middle of a sentence bold, because it matters. Which is right?
questions.3.options.0: <span class="bold"> — it gives it a name and it works
questions.3.options.1: <strong> — it means "this matters", while <span class="bold"> means nothing
questions.3.explain: <span> is for cases where no suitable tag exists — the price in a listing, say. A price is not "an important word", it is a price. But an important word has had its own tag for many lessons now.
questions.4.q: A page made of fourteen <div>s. It works flawlessly and with CSS it looks great. What is the real problem?
questions.4.options.0: It is slow — too many tags
questions.4.options.1: It says nothing. Neither Google, nor a screen reader, nor you in six months know what is what
questions.4.options.2: <div> is not supported by old browsers
questions.4.options.3: There is no problem, since it works
questions.4.explain: The code works flawlessly and says absolutely nothing. That is the difference between a page that displays and a page that is understood.
questions.5.q: A bike listing on a listings site. <article> or <section>?
questions.5.options.0: <section> — it is part of the listings list
questions.5.options.1: <article> — cut it out and paste it elsewhere and it still makes sense on its own
questions.5.explain: Ask: if I cut this out and put it on another site, would it make sense? A listing — yes. A section called "How to order" — no, meaningless without the page around it. The first is an article, the second a section.
questions.6.q: A blind visitor opens a page built entirely of <div>. What do they hear?
questions.6.options.0: Nothing — the reader cannot read a <div>
questions.6.options.1: A flat list: "group, group, text, text". No shape, no hierarchy, nothing to skip
questions.6.options.2: The same as with semantic tags — no difference
questions.6.options.3: Only the class names, read aloud
questions.6.explain: You skip the menu with a glance. They can only do it if you told them it is a menu. With semantic tags they hear "navigation, 3 links" and skip. Without them — they sit through all of it.
questions.7.q: The page heading is huge and beautiful — built with a <div> and three <span> in different colours. It looks exactly right. What is lost?
questions.7.options.0: Nothing — it looks exactly as intended
questions.7.options.1: Google does not know what the page is about, and a reader hears "text" instead of "heading level 1"
questions.7.explain: This is <div class="big-text"> — and it happens on real sites every day. The fix is not to remove the <span> tags; they are for the colours and they belong. The fix is an <h1> around them.
